title: Dizzy Dismukes

infobox.value: (1890-03-15)March 15, 1890 [Birmingham, Alabama](/wiki/Birmingham,_Alabama "Birmingham, Alabama"), U.S. | |
June 30, 1961(1961-06-30) (aged 71) [Campbell, Ohio](/wiki/Campbell,_Ohio "Campbell, Ohio"), U.S. | |

[Edit this at Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q5285112#P1825 "Edit this at Wikidata") | |
[Edit this at Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q5285112#P12331 "Edit this at Wikidata") | |

(March 15, 1890 – June 30, 1961) was an [American](/wiki/Americans "Americans") [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher") and [manager](/wiki/Manager_(baseball) "Manager (baseball)") in [Negro league baseball](/wiki/Negro_league_baseball "Negro league baseball") and during the pre-Negro league years. He was born in Birmingham, Alabama, where his father Isaac Lee Dismukes was a deacon in a Baptist church, and his mother Sallie taught Sunday School.[[9]](#cite_note-10) He knew from the time he was a youth that baseball was his first love, and he dropped out of school to follow his dream of becoming a baseball player.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

On December 16, 2020, Major League Baseball declared certain Negro leagues, from the span of 1920–1948, to be "Major League".[[1]](#cite_note-1) Dismukes' statistics reflect his time in the Negro leagues from 1920–1927, 1932–1933, and 1937–1938.

["MLB officially designates the Negro Leagues as 'Major League'"](https://www.mlb.com/press-release/press-release-mlb-officially-designates-the-negro-leagues-as-major-league). *MLB.com*. December 16, 2020. Retrieved December 14, 2024.

**William "Dizzy" Dismukes** (March 15, 1890 – June 30, 1961) was an [American](/wiki/Americans "Americans") [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher") and [manager](/wiki/Manager_(baseball) "Manager (baseball)") in [Negro league baseball](/wiki/Negro_league_baseball "Negro league baseball") and during the pre-Negro league years. He was born in Birmingham, Alabama, where his father Isaac Lee Dismukes was a deacon in a Baptist church, and his mother Sallie taught Sunday School.[[9]](#cite_note-10) He knew from the time he was a youth that baseball was his first love, and he dropped out of school to follow his dream of becoming a baseball player.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dizzy_Dismukes&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Career")]

Dismukes was a right-handed [submariner](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher"),[[11]](#cite_note-12) who is considered by many historians to be one of the best pitchers in the Negro leagues.

Born and raised in [Birmingham, Alabama](/wiki/Birmingham,_Alabama "Birmingham, Alabama"), he began his baseball career at age 17.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

Among his achievements as a pitcher, he defeated the then-major league champion Pittsburgh Pirates 2–1,[[13]](#cite_note-14) in an exhibition game in 1911.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

While a player, he periodically wrote about baseball for such black newspapers as the *Pittsburgh Courier*, beginning in the 1920s.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

Among the teams he played for were the Brooklyn (NY) Royal Giants,[[16]](#cite_note-17) Indianapolis ABCs and the St. Louis Stars.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

During his managing years, Dismukes became known for his wonderful memory during his playing and managing, and became known as a strategist. He is credited with teaching Webster McDonald and Carl Mays the tricks of submarine-style pitching.

He spent a number of years with the Kansas City Monarchs, in such roles as traveling secretary and business manager.[[18]](#cite_note-19) Later in his career, after major league baseball was integrated, he was a scout for the Chicago Cubs and then the New York Yankees.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

He joined the Yankees as a scout in 1953, having resigned his position as secretary of the Kansas City Monarchs.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

He died in 1961, at age 71, at the home of his sister in [Campbell, Ohio](/wiki/Campbell,_Ohio "Campbell, Ohio"); the cause of death was hardening of the arteries.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

At age 62, Dismukes received votes listing him on the 1952 *[Pittsburgh Courier](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Courier "Pittsburgh Courier")* player-voted poll of the Negro leagues' best players ever.[[22]](#cite_note-poll-23)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dizzy_Dismukes&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** On December 16, 2020, Major League Baseball declared certain Negro leagues, from the span of 1920–1948, to be "Major League".[[1]](#cite_note-1) Dismukes' statistics reflect his time in the Negro leagues from 1920–1927, 1932–1933, and 1937–1938.
